lessons.title: Range Concepts and Ranges Algorithms

background.label: C++17: STL Algorithms
background.body: STL algorithms before C++20 took iterator pairs: std::sort(v.begin(), v.end()). This required spelling out begin/end explicitly, offered no projection support, and produced verbose error messages when iterator requirements were violated. C++20 Ranges solve all three problems: algorithms accept a range directly, support projections, and express requirements through concepts.

lessons.motivation: Every time you write std::sort(v.begin(), v.end()), you are passing the same information twice — the compiler already knows that v is a vector with a beginning and an end. But the iterator-pair inconvenience is just the tip of the iceberg. Want to sort structs by a field? You need a five-line custom comparator. Violated an iterator contract? You get a three-screen error buried in template internals. C++20 Ranges eliminate all of this at once: algorithms accept a whole container, accept a projection instead of a comparator, and concepts make errors readable. A range is any type that has begin() and end(), and the concept std::ranges::range formally captures that.
lessons.theory:
**What a range is.** A range is any type T for which std::ranges::begin(r) and std::ranges::end(r) are valid. This includes vectors, arrays, strings, and user-defined containers — anything that can yield an iterator to its start and a marker for its end. The key C++20 extension: the end of a range can be a *sentinel* — a type different from the iterator type. For example, when traversing a C string the sentinel checks for the '\0' character rather than storing an end address. The concept std::ranges::range covers both cases and is the entry point into the range concept hierarchy.

**The range concept hierarchy.** Concepts are layered from weak to strong: input_range (single-pass — suitable for std::istream_iterator), forward_range (multi-pass, iterator is copyable — suitable for std::list), bidirectional_range (can go backwards — std::list and std::deque), random_access_range (O(1) by index — std::vector, std::deque, std::array), contiguous_range (elements are contiguous in memory — std::vector, std::array, std::string). Orthogonal to this chain: sized_range (has .size() in O(1)) and common_range (begin() and end() are the same type — for compatibility with older algorithms). The stronger the concept, the fewer types satisfy it, but the more operations become available.

**std::ranges algorithms.** A parallel set of algorithms lives in the std::ranges namespace inside \<algorithm\>. Three key differences from the old algorithms: (1) they accept the whole range — std::ranges::sort(v) instead of std::sort(v.begin(), v.end()); (2) they accept an optional *projection* argument after the comparator; (3) they return richer results — for example std::ranges::sort returns an iterator past the last element, and std::ranges::find_if returns a std::ranges::in_found_result with in and out fields. Concept constraints on arguments produce readable error messages instead of chains of "candidate template ignored".

**Projections.** A projection is a callable that maps a range element to the key used for comparison or searching. It is passed as the third argument to most ranges algorithms. It can be a lambda, a pointer to a member function, or a pointer to a data member. std::ranges::sort(team, {}, &Employee::salary) sorts by the salary field without writing a comparator — an empty object {} selects the default comparator (std::ranges::less), and the projection specifies what to compare. Projections are supported in sort, find, min_element, max_element, count_if, partition, and most other algorithms.

examples.title: Ranges Algorithms and Projections
examples.expectedOutput:
sorted: 1 2 3 5 8 9 
found 8: yes
by salary: Bob(75000) Carol(82000) Alice(90000) 
highest paid: Alice

examples.title: Range Concepts in Template Functions
examples.expectedOutput:
sum(vector): 28
sum(list):   60
median(vector): 4
count >= 4: 4
evens: 2 4 6 
vector is random_access: 1
list is random_access:   0

lessons.title: Views: filter, transform, take, drop, Pipe Syntax
lessons.motivation: Imagine you need to take even numbers from a vector, square each one, and print the first three results. Without views you either write three separate loops or copy data into auxiliary vectors at each step — wasting memory and time on intermediate results you never actually need. With C++20 views the whole pipeline fits on one line using the `|` operator and executes lazily: elements pass through every adapter one at a time, without copying, only when you actually read them. Views make code both expressive and efficient.
lessons.theory:
**What a view is.** A view is a lazy range adapter: it wraps an existing range and transforms iteration without copying or storing any data. Work happens element by element at iteration time — a view has no idea in advance how many elements you will request. Views are cheap to copy (O(1)) and satisfy the std::ranges::view concept. They do not own data: destroying the underlying container leaves the view dangling — an intentional constraint, not a design flaw.

**Core adapters.** std::views::filter(pred) passes only elements for which the predicate returns true. std::views::transform(f) applies function f to each element and yields the result. std::views::take(n) limits traversal to the first n elements. std::views::drop(n) skips the first n elements. std::views::take_while(pred) yields elements while the predicate is true and stops at the first failure. std::views::drop_while(pred) skips elements while the predicate is true, then yields the rest. All these adapters live in \<ranges\> and are accessible through the std::views namespace.

**Pipe syntax.** The `|` operator lets you chain adapters left to right. The expression `v | views::filter(f) | views::transform(g) | views::take(3)` creates one composite view — without performing any computation. Each `|` simply wraps the previous view in the next adapter. Computation happens only during traversal: in a range-for loop, when passed to an algorithm, or when the iterator is explicitly dereferenced. The result reads like prose left to right: "take v, keep only f, apply g, take the first three".

**Laziness and its consequences.** Laziness means a view never looks at elements beyond what is needed. If you take the first odd element via `filter | take(1)`, traversal stops as soon as it is found — the remaining elements are never examined. The most important consequence: views work with infinite ranges. std::views::iota(0) generates 0, 1, 2, … without bound, and that is safe precisely because no one requests all elements at once. Piping take(5) onto it yields exactly five numbers — no hang, no allocation.

examples.title: Core View Adapters
examples.expectedOutput:
evens: 2 4 6 8 10 
squared: 1 4 9 16 25 
middle 3 (drop 3, take 3): 4 5 6 
take while < 6: 1 2 3 4 5

examples.title: Lazy Pipeline and Infinite Range with iota
examples.expectedOutput:
pipeline result: 8 10 18 12 
first 5 squares of evens: 4 16 36 64 100 
iota(1, 6): 1 2 3 4 5
